sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 44-year-old female with a medical record number of 113322, presents six weeks post-right medial unicompartmental arthroplasty.
She reports being "all right" and states that her knee is "good" with no problems, except for numbness on the side of the knee.
She expresses happiness with the surgery and reports no pain.
She mentions that she is still early in her recovery and is doing physical therapy to work on strength, range of motion, and endurance.
She is concerned about returning to work, particularly as a UPS driver, and is asked about her job accommodations.
Past medical history:
[NOT MENTIONED]

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Her gait was antalgic with a slight limp and decreased velocity, without the use of an assistive device.
The right knee focused exam revealed a healed surgical scar, mild swelling, and tenderness on the area of the incision.
Range of motion is zero to 120 degrees, with everything else normal.
Test Results: Radiographs of the right knee, taken today, show implants in a stable position with no evidence of loosening or early complications.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is a 44-year-old female six weeks post-right medial unicompartmental arthroplasty with a well-healed surgical site and stable implants.
Problem list: Right knee unicompartmental arthroplasty; numbness on the side of the knee; status unknown.
Plan:
I instructed the patient to continue with physical therapy to work on strength, range of motion, and endurance.
I also advised her to avoid lifting greater than five or 10 pounds and not to stand or sit for longer than a half an hour.
I will write a note to excuse her from work for another six weeks.
I will schedule a telemedicine visit in six weeks to check on her progress and discuss her job accommodations further.
I informed her that she can return to driving when she feels safe to do so.
Follow-up:
The patient will return for a telemedicine visit in six weeks.
